coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots on paper for determination of Cu2+ in sugar cane spirits samples for fluorescence digital image-based method
coredata.dc:creator: Maia, Matheus Valentin
Suarez, Willian Toito
dos Santos, Vagner Bezerra
de Almeida, João Paulo Barbosa
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we proposed for the first time a simple and low-cost fluorometric method based on digital image using a UV-LED chamber and a 3D platform made of biodegradable polyacid lactic (PLA) and carbon dots (CDs) obtained from rice functionalized with cuprizone for the determination of Cu2+ in sugar cane spirits, using a paper-based device. The synthesized CDs were characterized by the techniques of UV–Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorometry, dynamic light scattering, scanning electron microscopy, zeta potential. The CDs obtained using a green hydrothermal method from biomass proved to be selective for the determination of Cu2+ when functionalized with bis (cyclohexanone) oxaldihidrazone (CPZ). The mechanism of detection was based on the quenching of fluorescence emitted by the CDs due to the Cu2+. For excitation of the CDs/CPZ, a chamber was built with four UV-LED as radiation source with maximum emission at 370 nm. An opening at the top allows the smartphone to detect the fluorescence emitted by the CDs. The captured image was decomposed to red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values. The determination of Cu2+ in the sugar cane spirits samples was obtained in a linear range from 2.00 to 7.22 mg L−1 with LD of 0.23 mg L‑1 for B channel with R2 = 0.9993. Based on the recovery data from 87.60% to 112.4% the matrix effect was not found. Samples of sugar cane spirits were analyzed by the fluorescence digital image method and the atomic absorption spectrophotometry, and there are no significant differences for 95% confidence of the data obtained, and thus, the method developed was accurace for Cu2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu2+
Paper-based device
Digital image analysis
Carbon dots
Nanoparticles
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Apparatus and software used to capture fluorescence RGB data
Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ employing CDs/CPZ
CDs characterization and fluorescence stability study
Quantitative analysis by the proposed method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for financial support provided by FAPEMIG (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais), CAPES (Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior) and CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico, grant number, 421147/2018-0) and FACEPE (Fundação de apoio à Pesquisa do Estado de Pernambuco (grant number, APQ 0413-1.06/21)).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-LED chamber developed for the fluorescence digital image-based method.
Difference between the relative fluorescent intensities of CDs with different ions in the ratio 1:100.
Fourier transform infrared spectra of CPZ, CDs/CPZ and CDs solutions previously dried.
Relative fluorescent intensities of CDs/CPZ regards to other ions in the ratio 1:100.
Analytical curves for Cu2+ determination using different channels of the RGB model.

Colors registered by the Color Name app and extracted from the digital image obtained using the smartphone. The B channel values are presented. Limit of detection (LD), Limit of quantification (LQ) and coefficient of determination (R2).
Determination of Cu2+ in cachaça by the F-DIB and reference methods (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots on paper for determination of Cu2+ in sugar cane spirits samples for fluorescence digital image-based method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Viçosa - UFV, Centro de Ciências Exatas e Tecnologia, Viçosa, MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Ciências Exatas e Tecnologia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Viçosa - UFV, Centro de Ciências Exatas e Tecnologia, Viçosa, MG, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fundamental Chemistry Department, Federal University of Pernambuco - UFPE, Recife, PE, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fundamental Chemistry Department, Federal University of Pernambuco - UFPE, Recife, PE, Brazil

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry Department, Federal University of Viçosa, Av. Peter Henry Rolfs, s/n° Cidade Universitária, Minas Gerais, MG - CEP 36.570-900, Brazil.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: s/n° Cidade Universitária, Minas Gerais, MG - CEP 36.570-900

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, we proposed for the first time a simple and low-cost fluorometric method based on digital image using a UV-LED chamber and a 3D platform made of biodegradable polyacid lactic (PLA) and carbon dots (CDs) obtained from rice functionalized with cuprizone for the determination of Cu2+ in sugar cane spirits, using a paper-based device. The synthesized CDs were characterized by the techniques of UV–Vis spectrophotometry, spectrofluorometry, dynamic light scattering, scanning electron microscopy, zeta potential. The CDs obtained using a green hydrothermal method from biomass proved to be selective for the determination of Cu2+ when functionalized with bis (cyclohexanone) oxaldihidrazone (CPZ). The mechanism of detection was based on the quenching of fluorescence emitted by the CDs due to the Cu2+. For excitation of the CDs/CPZ, a chamber was built with four UV-LED as radiation source with maximum emission at 370 nm. An opening at the top allows the smartphone to detect the fluorescence emitted by the CDs. The captured image was decomposed to red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values. The determination of Cu2+ in the sugar cane spirits samples was obtained in a linear range from 2.00 to 7.22 mg L−1 with LD of 0.23 mg L‑1 for B channel with R2 = 0.9993. Based on the recovery data from 87.60% to 112.4% the matrix effect was not found. Samples of sugar cane spirits were analyzed by the fluorescence digital image method and the atomic absorption spectrophotometry, and there are no significant differences for 95% confidence of the data obtained, and thus, the method developed was accurace for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Sugar cane spirits or Cachaça is genuinely a Brazilian alcoholic beverage, considered a national product, with great economic, social and cultural importance [1,2]. It is a drink that has unique sensory characteristics, produced from sugar cane through the fermentation process followed by a distillation step, characterized by the Ministry of Agriculture Livestock and Supply (MAPA) as a typical and exclusive drink of the brandy of sugar cane with an alcoholic percentage of 38 to 48% (v/v) at 20 °C [3]. Generally, the presence of Cu2+in cachaças is associated with non-compliance with good practices in the production process, such as the lack of cleaning of the alembic used in the distillation process [4], with the presence of Cu2+ on the surface of the alembic, during the cachaça distillation process, basic copper carbonate (Cu2CO3(OH)2) is formed on the metal surface, this carbonate is solubilized by the acid vapors produced during the distillation leading to contaminate the distillate [5].

Even though it takes a considerable amount of Cu2+ to cause any harm to health (for an adult the maximum amount allowed is 1000 µg/day), there are dysfunctions related to the accumulation of this ion in the body. The main pathology related to excessive Cu2+ intake is Wilson's Disease. This disorder is clinically characterized by hepatic and neurological manifestations due to the accumulation of Cu2+ in the liver and limbus of the cornea [6,7].

A potential aggravating factor for the presence of Cu2+ is its association with the formation of ethyl carbamate, a known carcinogen [2]. Brazilian legislation limits the Cu2+ content in distilled beverages to 5 mg L−1 and for the European Union (EU) 2 mg L−1 [3,8].

Many methods were developed for determination of Cu2+ in sugar cane spirits, such as, flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) suggested by the AOAC (Association of Official Analytical Chemists) [9], fast sequential atomic absorption spectrometry (FS-AAS) [10], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS) [11], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [12], and potentiometry [13]. Nowadays, other analytical methods focusing on portability, ease-to-use, fast analysis, low-cost and low waste generation have been developed, such as the digital image method [14].

The digital image based (DIB) method employs image obtained from the devices such as webcams, cameras, smartphones [15], which aims to obtain images of colorimetric reactions [16] and, recently fluorescence [17,18] as the F-DIB (fluorescence digital image-based). With specific applications or software, the images obtained through these devices are decomposed into color values using models such as RGB (Red, Green and Blue), CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (Black), HLS (hue, lightness and saturation) [19]. The color values obtained after the decomposition of digital images are intrinsic to the intensity of the color or fluorescence captured by the digital device, and it is possible to relate these values to the concentration of the analyte [20]. For DIB or F-DIB methods, ceramic [15,16], paper devices [17,19], polymers [18,20], are some used materials to construct the support platform to insert the sample to be analyzed.

Paper is a versatile, flexible, porous and eco-friendly substrate that is used in the manufacture of low-cost sensors and devices for rapid detection [15,16]. Paper-based sensors had been applied to food quality [20], environmental and pathogen detection [21] which makes an attractive analytical strategy for limited resources laboratories. Several techniques are used to manufacture these paper-based sensors, such as photolithography [22], wax printing [23], poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) [24], inkjet printing [25], laser cutting [26] among others. These devices are quite versatile in that they can detect a wide range of analytes, such as glucose, ketones [27], uric acid [28], proteins [29], nitrite [30], (L)-glutamate [31].

Carbon-based nanoparticles as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon dots (CDs) were discovered in 2004 by Xu et al [32–34]. Currently, CDs have become one of the great protagonists of the nanoscience of carbon-based materials. Even though there may be different subtypes of CDs, all are mostly carbon, ranging from 50% to 80% of their composition. The CDs can present functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH) and amine (–NH2) [35,36]. Similarly to other fluorescent materials, the fluorescence emitted by CDs is conditioned to several factors, such as the effect of the solvent, the presence of ions, pH medium, excitation wavelength, quantum efficiency, absorptivity, temperature, viscosity of the medium, paramagnetic materials [37–41].

In this work, a simple, low-cost and portable F-DIB employing a smartphone was developed to measure the quenching of fluorescence due to the interaction of the CDs with the Cu2+ present in cachaça samples. For this, the CDs were synthesized from rice starch, and then functionalized with bis (cyclohexanone) oxaldihidrazone (CPZ) and placed in a paper analytical device (PAD) for selective determination of Cu2+. Digital image acquisitions were performed in a chamber with four UV-LED with maximum emission at 370 nm with a smartphone coupled to it to capture the fluorescence image that was further treated using the RGB model.

The solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (resistivity > 18.0 MΩ cm) obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system (USA). Cuprizone (bis(cyclohexanone) oxaldihydrazone), CuSO4·5H2O, NaOH, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), HCl 37% (m/v) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The solutions of metallic ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr2+ Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2− were prepared from their analytical grade nitrate salts or chloride salts, all purchased from Vetec (Brazil). The stock solution of de Cu2+ 50 mg L−1 was prepared by dissolving 0.196 g of CuSO4·5H2O in 1 L of distilled water. Then, the Cu2+ stock solution was standardized by complexometric titration using EDTA. Rice used as a precursor of carbon nanoparticles and samples of non-aged cachaça were purchased in supermarkets in the city of Viçosa-MG-Brazil.

The fluorescence emission spectra were carried out using a quartz cuvette with four polished sides and 1.0 cm of optical path by an Avaspec-HS2048XL spectrofluorometer from Avantes (Netherlands) with CCD detector ranged from 200 − 1160 nm with a resolution between 1 and 10 nm. Moreover, a FluoroMax® spectrofluorometer from Horiba (France) with ozone-free xenon arc lamp, 150 W power with a detection based on photomultiplier R928P at a range of 185–850 nm was also used. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer from the Perkin-Elmer (USA) using a quartz cuvette with an optical path of 1.0 cm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was performed on an EM 109 microscope Zeiss (Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique experiments were performed on a Varian 660-IR from the PIKE (USA) at a spectral range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The zeta potential and the size of the CDs were measured by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using a Zetasizer Nano S (England). Moreover, the particle size was evaluated at an angle of 173° with 11 runs of 10 min.

Carbon nanoparticles were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment from the rice starch as a precursor. The starch was extracted using a solid–liquid extraction of 30 g of rice with 20 mL of distilled water until to observe the turbidity of water. Furthermore, a 15 mL aliquot of the starch-containing solution was added to a Teflon flask hermetically accommodated in a stainless-steel autoclave operated at 200 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace Fig. S1. The aqueous suspension containing the CDs was centrifuged and filtered using a filter (PES Millipore®) with pore size of 0.22 µm.

A set of experiments was conducted aiming to identify the effect of temperature and synthesis time on the fluorescent intensity of CDs. A central composite design of 2 factors with 11 experiments with 3 experiments at a central point was performed and used to obtain the response surface (RMS) and to find the relationship between temperature and time with the best conditions for the synthesis, Table S1.

In order to be more selective of the CDs to determine Cu2+, the nanoparticles synthesized were functionalized by adding 0.1 g of CPZ in 10 mL of the solution containing the CDs and left under reflux for 1 h. After this, the solution was washed with ethyl acetate to remove excess of CPZ and other synthesis products, and then, filtered using a filter (PES Milipore) with pore size of 0.22 µm.

To evaluate the effect of the potential interferences in the signal of fluorescence emitted by the CDs a study was carried out with the following ions Hg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr2+, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2−, under identical conditions in a 1:100 ratio of analyte/interferent and the effects on the fluorescence response was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus and software used to capture fluorescence RGB data
ce:section.ce:para: Digital images of the fluorescent solutions were obtained in a chamber of 23 × 15 × 11 cm dimensions using paperboard. In the chamber four light-emitting diodes (LED) emitting at a maximum wavelength of 370 nm (UV-LED) with a power of 10 W each were inserted Fig. 1 . A power supply operated at 60 Hz, 110–220 V with output of 12 V supplying a continuous current of 10 A was used. Optionally, a battery with similar setup could be used for portable applications. More information about the UV-LED and its emission spectra could be obtained in our previous work [18]. The RGB data were obtained with the free app Color Name using 30 × 30 pixels obtained from a smartphone iPhone 5S with an image without shadows or excess of brightness to avoid spurious signals [15].

In order to improve the organization, distribution and accommodation of PAD into the UV-LED chamber and to avoid contamination through capillarity effect, a 3.0 × 3.0 × 0.5 cm platform made of polylactic acid (PLA) in a 3D printer was produced. The 3D platform was generated using a 3D printer Creality® Ender 3 Pro, using the Ultimaker Cura 4.5 slicer. Each one of the 25 cavities has a design of an overlapping of a cylinder and a truncated cone [18]. To produce the PAD, a filter paper composed of a mixture of esters with a pore size of 0.45 µm manufactured by Whatman® was chosen. The PAD produced was placed in the 3D platform, a paper with hydrophobic characteristics was used in order to reduce the dispersion capacity of the sample, Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ employing CDs/CPZ
ce:section.ce:para: Using the app Color Name analytical curves were built to determine Cu2+ using the apparatus and the proposed methodology. For this, a stock solution of Cu2+ was prepared and after diluted using a solution of CDs/CPZ aiming to prepare six different concentrations of Cu2+ at a range from 2.00 and to 13.24 mg L−1. For all experiments, a 15 µL aliquot of the CDs/CPZ content Cu2+ were placed in the PAD for each analysis. For comparative purposes, the atomic absorption spectrometry method was used in order to obtain the accuracy of the F-DIB method employing the paired t-Test on the data of Cu2+ concentration obtained from cachaça samples.

Recovery assays were established to verify the presence of matrix effects on the determination of Cu2+ in five commercial cachaças samples. The study was carried out at two concentration levels, i.e., 2.5 and 6.0 mg L−1 with n = 3, according to the Eq. (1) where C1, C2 and C3 are the concentration of the fortified samples, the non-fortified sample and added concentration, respectively.
(1)
Recovery
( % )
=
C 1 - C 2
C 3
x 100

The experimental design was proposed to find the best reaction conditions for the synthesis of CDs. Thus, two important variables for the synthesis of nanoparticles were investigated in the process, temperature and heating time. The experimental tests and the data from the analysis of variance (ANOVA) with the statistical parameters of the quadratic correlation are presented in Table S2 and Table S3, respectively. Higher value of F and the lower value of P are required. According to Table S3, the quadratic and linear factors for temperature were significant (p < 0.05) with a R2 = 0.99513, which indicates the greatest validity for the predicted fluorescence values. An adjusted R2 coefficient of 0.98914 was obtained which indicates that the prediction agrees with the experimental results. A way to graphically express the factor interactions for a better demonstration of the results is presented in the Pareto chart in Fig. S3 by the significance of the effects. According to the 3D response surface, it is possible to note the high significance of temperature and not significance of the time of CD synthesis for both models, the linear and quadratic model at a confidence level of 95%, Fig. S4. It’s possible to observe the increase in the fluorescent intensity of the CDs when the temperature of the synthesis of CDs is higher and an insignificant increase when there is an increase in time of the synthesis. Thus, the synthesis via hydrothermal treatment of CDs at a temperature of 230 °C is the one that leads to greater fluorescent intensity, regardless of time. However, a temperature of 200 °C and a time of 6 h was chosen, once temperatures above 200 °C caused the deformation of the Teflon container as well as the locking of the equipment inside the autoclave.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs characterization and fluorescence stability study
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, CDs have absorption in the UV region extending outside the visible range. The presence of the CDs synthesized by different methods/processes could be noted by an absorption band ranging from 260 to 320 nm [35,42], and absorption at longer wavelengths can be extended through surface functionalization [43]. Due to the heterogeneity of CDs, it is difficult to accurately assign electronic transitions to bands, however it is known that electronic transitions can be of the nature n-π* for C = O, N–H, N-C, O–H bonding, or π-π* for C = C aromatic bonds [44]. Thus, an absorption band with maximum round 280 to 290 nm was observed and identified in the UV/Vis spectrum, Fig. S5. To characterize the nanoparticles in relation to their ability to absorb and emit radiation, the spectrofluorescence method was used. For this, the excitation wavelengths ranged from 350 and 390 nm and the fluorescence spectra were collected, Fig. S6. The Stock's displacement was observed and spectra were shifted to from higher wavelengths being this behavior is typical for CDs [32–34]. Therefore, for the F-DIB the UV-LED chamber was constructed using four high brightness UV-LED of power 10 W each with a maximum emission wavelength of 370 nm to be used as an excitation source.

The Morphology of the CDs were evaluated by TEM and the image is presented in Fig. S7. Based on the image, the spherical shape of the nanoparticles and its reduced size in a few nanometers is noted. To certify this small diameter of the CDs the particle size distribution using the DLS technique was performed and the nanoparticles presented an average size of 1.0 nm [45,46], Fig. S8. The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated in the pH range from 3 to 9. In Fig. S9, it is observed that the fluorescence is weakly dependent on the pH at a range from 3 to 5, with a decrease in the fluorescence for pH 6. Thus, pH 5 was used for further experiments. However, the CDs synthesized can have application potential in environments with different pHs. In addition, due to the cachaça samples presented commonly pH hanging from 4 to 5 [47–49], the fluorescence of CDs allows a good sensitivity to determine Cu2+.

It was found that the mean zeta potential for the CDs at pH 5 was − 7.81 mV. This potential indicates the desprotation of the hydroxyl groups and carboxylic acids due to the pH medium. Moreover, due to low surface charge, the CDs are stable and do not undergo aggregation that could lead to the formation of large and unstable particles.

In order to evaluate the stability of fluorescence emitted by the CDs, the solutions containing the nanoparticles were irradiated for 1 h at a fixed excitation wavelength of 370 nm, Fig. S10. The fluorescence of the CDs were measured for each 5 min. The CDs synthetized were able to maintain stable luminescence along this time. Moreover, the fluorescence of the nanoparticles was also evaluated when subjected to an increase in the ionic strength of the medium and the effect of a quenching substance. For this, a study was carried out at a pH of 5 and an increasing addition of NaCl concentration, varying from 0 to 1 mol L−1 and measuring the fluorescence. Supported by results presented in Fig. S11, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs did not change with the increase in the ionic strength and the presence of Cl−, indicating a good stability of the CDs synthetized. Certainly cuprizone must have prevented chloride ions from accessing the functional groups at the carbon quantum dots responsible for its fluorescence.

Initially, the feasibility of using CDs without any modification in their structure by functionalization with CPZ for the determination of Cu2+ was studied. This proposal was based on the quenching of the fluorescence of CDs as a function of the presence of Cu2+ and foreign species aiming to check its selectivity. Fig. 2 shows the possible effect of quenching of the fluorescence for solutions of various metal ions at the ratio 1:100, using non-functionalized CDs. It is observed that there was not a significant quenching effect of the fluorescence of the CDs except for the Hg2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions. In order to obtain and enhance the selectivity of CDs, a functionalization of the surface of the nanostructure with CPZ was performed, being CPZ one of the most used reagents for the selective determination of Cu2+.

Regarding the interaction between CPZ and nanoparticles based on CDs, it is observed that CPZ can interact with CDs through hydrogen bonds between the N–H, and C = O, and C = N functional groups of the CPZ with hydrogens of the hydroxyl groups, and C = O present in the structure of nanoparticles of the CDs, Fig. 3 . From the infrared spectrum, there is a decrease in the intensity of the symmetric stretching band of the N–H bond of CPZ, when it interacts with the nanoparticles, an evidence of the interaction by hydrogen bonds. The bands of N = O and C = N bonds in CPZ also suffered a decrease in intensity when the chelator (CPZ) interacts with the nanoparticles, thus evidencing the interaction between them, via hydrogen bonds. One aspect that deserves to be highlighted is that, in all cases, there is a small shift in the wavenumber to smaller values, when new bonds are established, this because, the formation of hydrogen bonds, between the hydrogens of the hydroxyls of the CDs with the electronegative atoms of the CPZ functional groups leading to alterations in vibrations of the N–H, C = O and C = N bonds. This behavior is characteristic of the functionalization process, similar results are reported in the literature [14,50,51].

The broad band of hydroxyls observed at 3396 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of CDs was practically subtle when it interacted with CPZ, meaning that hydrogen bonds may have been established between them. Based on the infrared spectrum of CDs/CPZ the interaction between them was effective, if compared with the spectra of CDs and CPZ. A possible structure for the functionalization of CDs with CPZ and possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs/CPZ by Cu2+ is presented in Fig. S12. The CD and CPZ interact by H-bond and the interaction between Cu2+ ions and N atoms of the CPZ molecule, whose non-bonding electron pairs are available.

After CD/CPZ functionalized, the study of quenching of the fluorescence due to the foreign species was evaluated with regards to the selectivity of the Cu2+ detection. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the functionalization resulted in a significant reduction in the interference of Hg2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions against Cu2+ compared to non-functionalized CDs (Fig. 2 and Fig. 4). In terms of selectivity, the functionalization of CDs decreased the interference of all tested ions, thus, showing greater efficiency of the proposed method with functionalization. The functionalized CDs were able to maintain stable fluorescence along the time, Fig S13.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis by the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Using the Color Name app, analytical curves were built to the determination of Cu2+. As can be seen in Fig. 5 , channel B presented the best correlation between fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration. The channel B presented a lower linearity compared to the other channels; however, it was used for Cu2+ detection due to the high sensitivity. In fact, the CD/CPZ presented a more intense fluorescence at around 450 nm. However, due to the wide band of emission, other photons were detected in the green (less intensity compared to 450 nm) and the red channel, the lower intensity. This result corroborates to that presented in the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. S6.

The following linear equation was obtained from Fig. 5 using B channel in the linear range between 2.00 and 7.22 mg L−1: FI = (-7.4823 ± 0.14) [Cu2+] + (252.47 ± 0.62) where FI is the fluorescence intensity and [Cu2+] is the Cu2+ concentration given in mg L−1. In Table 1 are presented some figures of merits of the F-DIB method developed.

From the data of the analytical curve, it was possible to calculate the detection and quantification limits of the method. The LD was calculated using: 3 × SD/ S, and LQ = 10 × SD/ S, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank and S is the slope of the analytical curve. The detection limit for the method using the B channel was 0.23 mg L−1 and the quantification limits of 0.62 mg L−1.

In Table S4 is presented the results obtained for the recovery study for Cu2+ determination in cachaça samples in triplicate. Supported by data presented in Table S4 the recovery was from 87.60% to 112.4%, and thus, all values obtained for Cu2+ recovery were satisfactory and no significant matrix effects were found in the analyzed samples.
For the analysis of Cu2+ in cachaça, five unaged commercial cachaça samples were analyzed using the F-DIB and the reference method. The results obtained are shown in Table 2 .

In order to compare the results achieved through the two methods, the paired T-test with 95% confidence level was carried out. The aforementioned test indicated a p-value of 0.95 for the Blue channel was higher than the 0.05 significance value, thus indicating that there is no difference between the data obtained at a 95% confidence level for both methods. Moreover, the proposed method is adequate to reach the limits suggested to the legislation for the Cu2+ content in distilled beverages, i.e. 5 mg L−1 according to Brazilian legislation and 2 mg L−1 to the European Union [3,8]. To use the F-DIB method, it is necessary to train the analyst, however, because of its simplicity and its ease-to-use, this procedure is easy. In addition, the low cost of its apparatus as well as its maintenance, makes it easy to construct, making it accessible related to conventional spectrophotometer or AAS spectrometers. Another positive aspect of the F-DIB method proposed here is its portability, and its low weight make it possible to be operated in a battery module. Regarding methods for determining Cu2+ in cachaça that employ digital image analysis, the present work is advantageous in some aspects, mainly for selectivity to determine Cu2+ and instantaneous response when it is compared to others found in the literature [2,14,16,52], as can be seen in Table S5.

A F-DIB method employing a paper-based device coupled to a 3D platform to detect Cu2+ in cachaça was developed, which, due to its portability together with the UV-LED chamber and smartphone, can be taken to field analysis beyond the laboratories. Furthermore, the validation parameters evaluated presented satisfactory results when compared to the AAS method. The F-DIB method exhibits advantages for its simplicity of operation and for being sensitive enough to quantify Cu2+ in non-aged cachaça. For the first time in the literature, carbon nanoparticles functionalized with CPZ for the determination of Cu2+ in cachaça using a paper device coupled to a 3D platform for F-DIB analysis was developed. In addition, the proposed method corroborates with the precepts of green chemistry employing CDs obtained from rice starch, a renewable source, and the hydrothermal synthesis without adding any toxic reagent and the PLA 3D platform is biodegradable. The only reagent used was the CPZ, however it was used in small quantities. The low amount of reagent consumption, as well as the small amount of sample, results in only 15 µL of residue per analysis.

Matheus Valentin Maia: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Willian Toito Suarez: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Vagner Bezerra dos Santos: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. João Paulo Barbosa de Almeida: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support provided by FAPEMIG (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais), CAPES (Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior) and CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico, grant number, 421147/2018-0) and FACEPE (Fundação de apoio à Pesquisa do Estado de Pernambuco (grant number, APQ 0413-1.06/21)).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107500.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
